text: Training history accounts for the greatest variance in marathon finish time among anthropometric, training, and pacing variables combined.
Setting a pragmatic (realistic) target finish time based on training and previous race performances is the second most important predictor of marathon performance.
Greater in-race pace variance (less even pacing) is significantly associated with slower marathon finish times.
Marathon experience (number of previous marathons completed) is a statistically significant but relatively small predictor of marathon finish time.
A combined model of age, BMI, marathon experience, training history, target finish time pragmatism, and in-race pace variance explains approximately 86% of the variance in marathon finish time.
Age and BMI do not make statistically significant independent contributions to marathon finish time prediction when training history, pacing, and goal-setting variables are included in the model.
